coredata.dc:description: Imidazolium bromide salts have been shown as potent antibiotic molecules that show structure-based bioactivity related to their cation alkyl side chain length. To enhance the bioavailability of lipophilic alkyl side chains herein, a 1,8-naphthalimide group containing imidazolium bromide salts bearing different lengths of alkyl chains (NIM1, 2, and 3) are coupled with fluorescent carbon dots (C-NIMs) through electrostatic and pi-pi interactions. Further, obtained nanocarriers were radio-labeled with iodine-131 (131I) to track the bacterial uptake of them by Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Antibacterial activities were also investigated by the microdilution method. Comparison studies showed that both radiolabeling efficiency and lipophilicity increased when NIMs were integrated onto the CDots. More importantly, CDots resulted in 4-fold enhanced uptake of NIM1 by S. aureus bacterium as compared to pristine imidazolium bromide salts while at a higher number of alkyl chain lengths enhancement was 2-fold.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–vis absorption spectra of green-emitting CDots and blue-emitting C-NIMs formed with imidazolium bromide salts bearing different lengths of the alkyl chain (inset: fluorescence photos of CDots and C-NIMS under UV light), TEM images of b) bare CDots, c) C-NIM1, d) C-NIM2 and e) C-NIM3.
FT-IR spectra of NIMs (a) and C-NIMs (b).
In vitro stability results of 131I-NIM1, 131I-NIM2 and 131I-NIM3.
In vitro stability results of 131I-C-NIM1, 131I-C-NIM2, and 131I-C-NIM3.

The radiolabeling yield of 131I-NIM1, 131I-NIM2, and 131I-NIM3 was determined as 88.21 ± 2.15 %, 63.7 ± 2.1 %, and 96.2 ± 2.1 % while the radiolabeling efficiencies of 131I-C-NIM1, 131I-C-NIM2, and 131I-C-NIM3 were determined as 85.12 ± 0.16 %, 83.15 ± 0.15 %, and 86.35 ± 0.28 %, at 24 h respectively. The radiolabeled C-NIMs and NIMs were determined to be quite stable for 24 h [56,57]. Moreover, C-NIM dispersions (512 μg/mL) prepared in ddH2O and PBS (pH 7.4, 10x) showed no aggregation or precipitation following two weeks of storage at room temperature.

a) Fluorescent images of Staphylococcus aureus cells after incubation at 37 °C for 1 h with 40 μg/mL CDot and C-NIMs. and fluorescence mode at excitation wavelengths 488 nm (green) with the bright field. Objective magnification 10x and 20 × . b) Fluorescent images of Escherichia coli cells after incubation at 37 °C for 1 h with 40 μg/mL CDot and C-NIMs. and fluorescence mode at excitation wavelengths 488 nm (green) with the bright field. Objective magnification 10x and 20 × .

Preparation of (a) green-emitting fluorescence CDots from degassed cola drink, and (b) post-integration of imidazolium salts to the carbon surface that result in blue-emitting C-NIMs.

head.ce:title: Enhanced bacterial uptake of 131I -labeled antimicrobial imidazolium bromide salts using fluorescent carbon nanodots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Imidazolium bromide salts have been shown as potent antibiotic molecules that show structure-based bioactivity related to their cation alkyl side chain length. To enhance the bioavailability of lipophilic alkyl side chains herein, a 1,8-naphthalimide group containing imidazolium bromide salts bearing different lengths of alkyl chains (NIM1, 2, and 3) are coupled with fluorescent carbon dots (C-NIMs) through electrostatic and pi-pi interactions. Further, obtained nanocarriers were radio-labeled with iodine-131 (131I) to track the bacterial uptake of them by Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Antibacterial activities were also investigated by the microdilution method. Comparison studies showed that both radiolabeling efficiency and lipophilicity increased when NIMs were integrated onto the CDots. More importantly, CDots resulted in 4-fold enhanced uptake of NIM1 by S. aureus bacterium as compared to pristine imidazolium bromide salts while at a higher number of alkyl chain lengths enhancement was 2-fold.

ce:section.ce:para: Imidazolium salts (IMS) have been used in a variety of areas due to their inherent merits such as ease of synthesis, biocompatibility, high chemical and thermal stability, high ionic conductivity, and a wide electrochemical window [1–6]. Furthermore, IMS can be easily isolated from the roots of Lepidium meyenii (maca) has significant antimicrobial potential and low toxicity against human cells [7–9]. The hydrophilic cationic segments of IMS have toxic effects on several micro-organisms including Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [10,11]. IMS show strong interactions with biological components such as cell membrane and DNA via ionic exchange mechanism. Besides, toxicity and bioactivity of IMS can be regulated by altering their amphiphilic features [12]. The cationic IMS have longer alkyl chains and are more lipophilic compounds, in this way they can pass through the cell membrane and cause cell damage [13]. However, the lipophilicity of them may decrease their bioavailability when given to the bloodstream due to possible aggregations in aqueous environments.

In recent years, nanotechnology has made great progress in the healthcare-focused research areas and technologies. Various nanoparticles such as silver, silver oxide, zinc oxide, titanium oxide, calcium oxide, copper oxide, gold, silica, and carbon have been reported as potent antimicrobial materials [14–20]. It has been known that carbon-based nanostructures such as single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and graphene oxide (GO) structures have the significant antimicrobial potential [21,22]. The antimicrobial efficiency of carbon-based nanoparticles is mostly based on the physical interaction of them with the bacteria. For this reason, the shape and surface area of carbon-based nanostructures are quite important for direct interaction between the bacteria and nanostructure [23,24]. Additionally, the high surface area of these materials with rich surface-active groups and aromatic residues allows improving surface properties. In a study reported by Park et al., carbon nanotubes and imidazolium-based ionic liquids were bonded with each other via a covalent interaction, and thus the solubility of the carbon nanotubes was remarkably increased [25].

Carbon nanodots (CDots) are new members of carbon-based nanomaterials and are considered as an alternative to semiconductive quantum dots in electronic, optical, catalytic, and biological application fields [26–28]. The interest in these materials is due to their many interesting properties such as biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, photostability, ease of surface functionalization with non-toxic precursors, as well as high photoluminescence (PL) behavior, which is rare in other carbon allotropes [29–32]. Recent studies showed that they could be used as a nanocarrier for various molecules for ensuring increased update of them into different types of cells [31,33,34], while recently, the antimicrobial and anti-fungal properties of ROS producing CDots were also reported [35,36]. CDots may contain reactive surface functional groups such as carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, and many others by simply the use of surface passivation agents [27,32,37,38]. There are several studies using polymers [39,40], lipids [40], ionic liquids [41,42], deep eutectic solvents [42], and ions for the surface functionalization of CDots. Therefore, CDots may serve as a molecular carrier for the imidazolium bromide salts in the cationic form, and through this immobilization, they could provide a high surface area which is suitable for intensifying these cationic densities for improved uptake of the NIMs while increasing the bioavailability by stabilizing the lipophilic alkyl chains through surface immobilization [43].

In the present study, the 1,8-naphthalimide group containing imidazolium bromide salts bearing different lengths of alkyl chains (NIM1, 2, and 3) were integrated onto the Cdots. Cdots carrying a valuable cargo on the surface were synthesized from degassed cola drink rich in sugar as the main carbon source for revalorization of selected commercial liquid waste as new precursors of “green” nanomaterial via a novel one-pot synthesis method. Imidazolium bromide salts (NIM1, 2, and 3) and CDots bearing these salts (C-NIM1, C-NIM2, and C-NIM3) were then radiolabeled with iodine-131 (131I) for tracking the bacterial uptake of NIMs in a Gram-positive bacterium, Staphylococcus aureus. Designed fluorescent and radiolabeled nanoparticles could have the potential to be used for multipurpose as antimicrobial nanocarrier, vehicles for radiotherapy, and dual radio/fluorescence tracking/imaging agents.

(1,8-naphthalic anhydride, 1-(3-aminopropyl) imidazole, 1-bromododecane, 1-bromohexadecane and 1-bromotetradecane, Iodogen) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck and used without further purification. The imidazolium bromide salts (NIM1, NIM2, or NIM3) and N-(3-propylimidazole)-1,8-naphthalene monoimide (Scheme 1 ) were prepared according to procedures previously reported in the literature [44–46].

Degassed Cola drink is a sugar-rich (fructose/glucose syrup and caramel) waste which as a result is a good carbon source for CDot synthesis where phosphoric acid and caffeine content of it results in highly fluorescent tiny nanoparticles [47]. To do so, a thermal synthesis procedure reported by Genc et al. was followed (Scheme 1a) [48]. The cola drink was first degassed by stirring in a magnetic stirrer overnight. Then, the obtained solution was mixed with water and ethanol mixture (1: 2: 2 v/v). The mixture was thermally treated for 35 min at 250 °C in a Teflon oven vessel. The resulting brown caramelized material was dispersed in ddH2O (4 mL). The suspension was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min, and nanosized CDots were collected from the supernatant taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of imidazolium salt integrated CDots (C-NIM1, C-NIM2, and C-NIM3)
ce:section.ce:para: 2.5 mg of the imidazolium salt (NIM1, NIM2, or NIM3) were dissolved in 500 μL of ethanol and sonicated for five minutes to obtain a homogenous solution. Then, the solution was mixed with 2.5 mg/mL CDot solution (1:1 v/v ratio) under sonication that was continued for a while for ensuring a homogenous mixing (Scheme 2b).

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra of CDots and C-NIMS were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800). The dried powders of CDots, NIMs, and C-NIMS were diluted with Mili-Q water to perform Attenuated total reflection (ATR)- Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Frontier Waltham, A 02451 USA), and spectrum GX spectrometry within the wave-number range of 400–4.000 cm−1. To eliminate the water interferences, all studies were done with a water background. Hydrodynamic particle radius was measured before and after surface modification using Dynamic Light Scattering technique (DLS) via the Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, UK. C-NIM specimens were observed under JEM– 1010 Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL, Japan)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiolabeling of NIMs and C-NIMs with 131I
ce:section.ce:para: The radiolabeling of NIMs and C-NIMs was performed via the iodogen method. Briefly, 25 μL of C-NIM (1.25 mg/mL) was diluted to 500 μL with distilled water, and also 1 mg of NIM was diluted to 1 mL of distilled water. NIM and C-NIM solutions were added into the iodogen coated (1 mg) tubes. Then, Na131I (9.25 MBq) was added to the mixture and incubated for 30 min. The control of the radiolabeling yield of NIMs and C-NIMs was determined by the thin layer radio chromatography (TLRC) method. After the incubation step, radiolabeled NIMs and C-NIMs have dropped onto the cellulose-coated plastic (ITLC-cellulose) sheets (1 × 10 cm; Merck). The TLC-cellulose sheets were developed in two different mobile phases [mobile phase-1: n-butanol/distilled water/acetic acid (4:2:1); mobile phase-2: isopropyl alcohol/n-butanol- 0.2 N NH4(OH)2 (2:1:1)]. Afterward, ITLC-cellulose sheets were removed from the mobile phases, dried, scanned by the TLC scanner (BioScan AR-2000 Washington DC, USA), and the radiolabeling efficiencies were determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Stability studies of NIMs and C-NIMs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the shelf life of radiolabeled NIMs and C-NIMs, in vitro stability studies were performed. The radiolabeled NIMs and C-NIMs moved to a clean tube, and the radiolabeling efficiencies of samples were determined at different time intervals (30, 60, 120, 240, and 1440 min) by using the TLRC method. Moreover, C-NIM dispersions of 512 μg/mL prepared in ddH2O and PBS (pH 7.4, 10x) for further evaluation of possible aggregation or precipitation in a time course of 2 weeks when stored at room temperature.

The n-octanol/water ratios were calculated to determine the lipophilicities of NIMs and C-NIMs. To analyze the lipophilicity, 100 μL of radiolabeled NIMs or C-NIMs was added to a solution containing 3 mL of n-octanol and 2.9 mL of water. Afterward, the solutions were stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 60 min, and the phases were separated by centrifugation (2500 rpm, 5 min). 500 mL of a sample from each phase were counted by Cd(Te)-RAD-501 single-channel analyzer. The average activity of the octanol phase was divided into the average activity of the aqueous phase to calculate the octanol-water partition coefficient (Po/w) values of radiolabeled NIMs and C-NIMs.

The antimicrobial activity of the NIMs and C-NIMs against Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 29213 and Escherichia coli ATCC 25922 was investigated by microdilution method. Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values were determined according to the European Committee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (EUCAST) recommendations. Bacteria were grown on Mueller-Hinton Agar (Merck) at 35 °C for 24 h, fresh colonies were taken by sterile swabs and suspended in sterile physiological saline. The turbidity of the suspensions was adjusted to 0.5 McFarland by densitometer device (Biosan, DEN-1). The suspensions were diluted 100-fold. Cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth (Merck) (50 μl) was added into each well of sterile 96-well microplates. NIMs and C-NIMs (50 μl) were added into the first wells, and the serial dilutions were performed (From 1–512 μg/mL). Bacterial suspensions (50 μl) were added to the wells, and the microplates were incubated for 16–20 h at 37 °C. The lowest concentration that inhibited the growth of the bacteria was defined as MIC value. Each sample was tested in triplicate. Gentamicin (Sigma) was used as a reference and the quality control ranges were evaluated according to EUCAST.

The zeta potential was measured by Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instrument, Malvern, UK) at room temperature (25 °C) with 90 degrees scattering angle. For this purpose, both bacterial suspensions were prepared from fresh bacterial cultures grown on Mueller-Hinton Agar (Merck) at 35 °C. S. aureus and E. coli (live) bacterial suspensions were used in the experimental set, and also measurements were taken from Heat-inactivated (dead) bacteria groups for control purposes. The suspensions obtained for the bacteria in each group were centrifuged (3300 rpm, 20 min) and washed with sterile water (three times) to remove media residue and clean bacterial cells before future analysis. Zeta potential measurements were carried out with 1 mM potassium phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4), which is the pH range we use in all experiments, to minimize any effect of pH and keep the conditions the same. After that, the turbidity of the suspensions was adjusted between 0.10 and 0.13 McFarland by densitometer device in 10 mL (measured at 590 nm). Each compound (NIM1, NIM2, NIM3, C-NIM1, C-NIM2, C-NIM3) was added to the bacterial suspensions, and zeta potential measurements were taken at 0, 2, 4 h. For zeta potential measurements, the bacterial suspension incubated with NIMs and CNIMs were vortexed before transferring the mixture 1 mL of a 1 mM PBS buffer to Malvern polystyrene U-shaped cell. Each of the experiments was carried out under the same experimental conditions (n ​​ = 3) [49,50].

Bacteria were grown on Mueller-Hinton Agar (Merck) at 35 °C for 24 h, fresh colonies were taken by sterile swabs and suspended in sterile physiological saline. Then, it was transferred to 15 mL falcon tubes to have a colony from each bacterial strain and the optical density was measured at 600 nm by adjusting it to 1.0 (108 cells per 1 mL). Then, these bacterial suspensions, which were transferred into falcon tubes in 5 mL each, were centrifuged at 2300 rcf for 20 min and washed twice with sterilized PBS (pH 7.4). 920 μL of both bacterial suspensions and 80 μL of C-NIMs were transferred to microcentrifuge tubes, the suspension suspended to 1 mL and lightly vortexed (concentration of C-dot and C-NIMs 2.5 mg / mL). The bacteria/C-NIM suspensions were kept at 37 °C for 1 h with gentle shaking in the water bath. After incubation, the C-NIMs-bacteria suspension was centrifuged to obtain pellets, the supernatant was removed, and the pellet was resuspended in PBS buffer again. This process is repeated twice to remove all unbound C-NIMs. Later, on slides prepared by dropping 100 μL of 5% agarose gel onto a slide, fluorescence imaging experiments were performed by dropping 50 μL of both bacteria suspensions [51]. The fluorescent images were taken by Smart Fluorescent Cell Analyzer Viewer Imager (NanoEntek Digital Bio JuLi, Korea).

The bacterial uptake studies of 131I-NIMs and 131I-C-NIMs have been performed against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 29213 strain and Gram-negative Escherichia coli ATCC 25922 strain and the obtained results were compared and discussed in detail. Briefly, the S. aureus strains (1.5 × 107 CFU ml−1) were suspended in sterile PBS (pH 7.4), and placed in an Eppendorf tube. The 131I labeled NIMs and C-NIMs (10 μCi for each Eppendorf tube) were added to the suspended S. aureus bacteria. The radioactivity of 131I labeled NIMs and C-NIMs in Eppendorf tubes were counted with Cd(Te) detector as cps (count per second), and the values were saved as A0. Then, the samples were incubated at 37 °C incubator for 1, 2, and 4 h. After the incubation periods, S. aureus and E. coli bacteria in Eppendorf tubes were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was removed. The pellet-like bacteria at the bottom of the Eppendorf tubes were washed 2 times with 1 mL of PBS. After each washing step, the total volume was completed to the initial volume by PBS addition. Then, the activity values were counted by the Cd(Te) detector (A1). Additionally, the experiment was performed with Na131I. The percentage uptake values of the radiolabeled compound and Na131I on S. aureus and E. coli were calculated using the
A 1
/
A 0
× 100
formula. All experimental steps were conducted under dark conditions to prevent additional light-induced changes.

Statistical analyses were performed by the GraphPad program. Student's t-test was used to evaluate the significance of differences in comparisons, differences between samples were considered statistically significant at p ≤ 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imidazolium salt integrated CDots (C-NIM1, C-NIM2, and C-NIM3): synthesis and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer was used to determine the surface functional groups of the prepared CDots and C-NIMs, and obtained results are shown in Fig. 2 . According to the FT-IR spectrum, The broad shoulder peaks observed at approximately 3600 cm−1 after surface modification are thought to be due to the OH groups found on the surface of the CDots. CO stretching bands appeared in the 1654 cm−1 range indicate the presence of hydroxyl groups and different modes of CO (1000−1400 cm−1) and CO alkyl groups (1150−1000 cm−1) presented on bare CDot surface (Fig. 2b). Moreover, the C-NIMs has been observed as two separate peaks vibration bands (1654 and 1700 cm-1) of naphthalene imide linking to the imidazolium cation, and this is proof of successful integration of NIMs onto the CDots (Fig. 2 a–b).

The radiolabeling of the samples was carried out by using the iodogen method. Na131I was oxidized with Iodogen, and oxidized iodine (131I+) was labeled with imidazolium salts through exchanging places with H+ in the aromatic ring [54,55].

The relative front values (Rf) were determined and shown in Table 1 . The difference of Rf values between Na131I and 131I labeled samples confirm that the radiolabeling was performed successfully. The radiolabeling efficiencies were increased when NIMs were presented on the CDots showing the positive effect of the high surface area of nanoparticles. The efficiency values determined were 89.99 ± 4.2 % for 131I-NIM1, 68.11 ± 5.3 % for 131I-NIM2, 91.33 ± 2.6 % for 131I-NIM3, 91.25 ± 0.65 % for 131I-C-NIM1, 90.35 ± 1.05 % for 131I-C-NIM2, and 93.45 ± 0.35 % for 131I-C-NIM3 by using TLRC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stability of NIMs and C-NIMs
ce:section.ce:para: The radiolabeling stability was evaluated by measuring the efficiencies of NIMs and C-NIMs at different time intervals, and the results were demonstrated in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 . It was observed that the radiolabeling yields of 131I-C-NIMs and 131I-NIMs show similar results over time. The radiolabeling yields were found to be quite high up to 240 min (85.5 ± 5.7 % for 131I-NIM1,% 61.5 ± 5.7 % for 131I-NIM2 and 93.5 ± 5.7 % for 131I-NIM3, 89.75 ± 0.75 % for 131I-C-NIM1, 89.95 ± 0.58 % for 131I-C-NIM2 and 90.15 ± 0.55 % for 131I-C-NIM3). The radiolabeling yield of 131I-NIM1,131I-NIM2 and 131I-NIM3 was determined as 80.21 ± 2.15 %, 60.7 ± 2.1 %, and 86.2 ± 2.1 % while the radiolabeling efficiencies of 131I-C-NIM1, 131I-C-NIM2and 131I-C-NIM3 were defined as 85.12 ± 0.16 %, 83.15 ± 0.15 %, and 86.35 ± 0.28 %, at 24 h respectively.

The Po/w values of the lipophilicities of 131I-NIM1, 131I-NIM2, and 131I-NIM3 were determined as 3.33 ± 0.31, 4.85 ± 0.56, and 5.18 ± 0.72, respectively. The values determined for 131I-C-NIM1, 131I-C-NIM2, and 131I-C-NIM3 were 1.02 ± 0.29, 1.05 ± 0.02, and 1.04 ± 0.24, respectively. NIMs are lipophilic compounds due to due to the long alkyl chains in the structures [13]. It was seen in our results that the imidazole structure having an alkyl chain of sixteen carbons (NIM3) has the highest lipophilicity. Furthermore, the lipophilicities of NIMs linked to carbon dots (C-NIMs) were found to show decreased lipophilicity with a similar trend as expected. The presence of CDots increased the dispersibility of NIMs in aqueous environments.

The MIC values of the NIMs and C-NIMs and comparison of MIC values are presented in Table 2 . C-NIM1 compound had the lowest MIC value against S. aureus strain (MIC: 1 μg/mL) in our experiments. The MIC values of NIM1 and C-NIM2 against S. aureus were determined as 2 μg/mL. Besides, NIM1 and C-NIM1 had the lowest MIC values against the E. coli strain, and the MIC values were determined as 4 μg/mL. The MIC values of the reference agent (gentamicin) were found as 0.5 μg/mL against S. aureus and E. coli strains, and the MIC values were in quality control ranges.

According to the results of the microdilution method, the MIC values of C-NIM1 and C-NIM3 were found to be 2-fold lower than the MIC values of NIM1 and NIM3 in S. aureus. Moreover, C-NIM2 was found to be more effective than the NIM2 compound, and the MIC of C-NIM2 was 4-fold lower than NIM2 against S. aureus. In parallel to the findings on S. aureus strain, the MIC of C-NIM2 was 2-fold lower than NIM2 against E. coli strain. As a result, studied CDots improved the antibacterial effect of the NIMs by decreasing the MIC values from 2-fold to 4-fold in S. aureus and E. coli in our experiments.

In this study, zeta potential values of all substances examined were found in both bacteria. The different cell walls of Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus are associated with the functional groups they carry. The different zeta potential values obtained can be explained by the partial destruction of the cell walls and changes in the conductivity of the cell membrane. The results presented in this study are consistent with the results reported in the literature suggesting that bacterial cell load changes depending on its physiological state. In the experiment, we prepared the E. coli and S. aureus suspensions in the same proportions. Different zeta values ​​can be obtained in the applied buffer, so we added dead versions of bacteria to the experiment set. We know from studies in the literature that the zeta potential of dead cells will be less negative than the zeta potential values ​​of living ones, and we used it to compare bacteria as a control group [50]. For this reason, the zeta potential was measured for dead and living bacteria. For this purpose, some living E. coli and S. aureus were killed by heat. Changes that occur due to the uptake of NIMs and C-NIMs within both bacteria cells caused a difference in zeta potentials. Surface neutralization of the membrane is important to evaluate the antimicrobial activity of substances acting on the bacterial surface [58]. In our study, the mean Zeta potential of untreated E. coli and S. aureus was found to be -15.9 and −7.20 mV, respectively. As can be seen in Table 3 and Table 4 , the zeta potential values ​​of living E.coli bacteria left to incubation with each substance at different periods become more negative by increasing, but it does not cause a significant change in dead E. coli. For example, zeta potential values ​​measured for E. coli as a result of incubation with C-NIM1 were found as -13.4 ± 1.91 at the 1 -h, -20.70 ± 1.12 at the 2 -h, and -17.83 ± 0.31 mV at the 4 -h. The presence of an additional negatively charged LPS layer in Gram-negative bacteria compared to Gram-positive bacteria is thought to have a higher negative potential in E. coli than S. aureus [59], and our results support this finding. As can be seen from our results, all the compounds applied caused slight changes in the zeta potential in E. coli or S. aureus in all incubation times compared to each other. S. aureus, significant changes in zeta potentials were observed when cells were exposed to NIM1 and C-NIM1. However, in E. coli significant changes in zeta potentials were observed when cells were exposed to NIM2 and C-NIM2. Besides, it was determined that the magnitude of the increase and decrease in the negativity of the zeta potential depends on time (up to 4 h). It was found that the positive surface potential of NIMS and C-NIMS interacts with the negative surface potential of the bacterial membrane and the potential shifts towards neutrality and this behavior changes with time. Such a change in potential can be associated with increased membrane permeability and uptake behavior [59,60]. Current observations have been shown by previous studies that surface charge neutralization leads to altered membrane permeability. Also, when studied at the same concentrations, it is related to the decrease in the negativity of the zeta potential, which is found more in S. aureus than in E. coli, and this result is consistent with the results in the literature [59]. Thus, the less variability of the Zeta potential in Gram-negative bacteria is thought to be due to the presence of higher anionic groups and O-antigen density in the LPS membrane, as well as the thin peptidoglycan layer that acts as an additional barrier. Thus, this layer in Gram-negatives helps to preserve the surface potential and thus protect the integrity of the membrane [61].

Each fluorescence image was taken with a Smart Fluorescent Cell Analyzer Viewer Imager. The device has two separate channels (bright and greenfield) as the light source as 488 nm excitation and 520 nm emission range White and Blue LED. Microscope magnification is explained as 4x for the objective and as digital magnification. This magnitude corresponds to 10x and 20x magnifications in microscopes, respectively. The camera features are explained by the manufacturer as 1.3 M pixels (1280 × 1024). This experiment was conducted with live strains of both bacterial species. In the literature, it has been emphasized that some of the CDots can selectively distinguish between dead and live bacterial strains [62]. In this study, priority was given to fluorescence imaging of live bacterial strains. Each C-NIM and CDot were separately visualized in both bacterial strains under equal conditions. As control groups, CDot and C-NIMs showed fluorescence in both live bacterial species. The fluorescence images obtained are shown in S. aureus and E. coli respectively (Fig. 5
a–b). Apart from this, the finding that we can say as a difference between the substances is that C-NIM2 showed fluorescence in more bacteria in both strains. It can be said that it would be more efficient to use C-NIM2 for fluorescence imaging among these substances.

The bacterial uptake results of C-NIMs and NIMs were compared, and the results were shown in Fig. 6–8
. The bacterial uptake of 131I-NIM1 and 131I-C-NIM1 was shown in Fig. 6, and the maximum uptake of 131I-NIM1 was determined as 3.76 ± 0.73 % and 10.12 ± 1.35 % for S. aureus and E. coli respectively at 1 h. However, the uptake of 131I-NIM1 began to decrease during the following periods in S. aureus while its stable in E. coli. At the same time, the uptake of 131I-C-NIM1 was doubled the 131I-NIM1 alone (7.65 ± 1.82 %) at 1 h. Furthermore, contrary to the pristine 131I-NIM1, the uptake of particle attached 131I-NIM1 increased by time and reached the value of 9.76 ± 1.47 % after 4 h of incubation in S. aureus. The highest uptake values were detected when C-NIM1 was applied to E. coli.

The bacterial uptake results of 131I-NIM2 and 131I-C-NIM2 are shown in Fig. 7. The highest uptake of 131I-NIM2 was determined as 2.68 ± 0.34 % at 1 h in S. aureus and decreases at the following hours. Contrary to 131I-NIM2, the uptake of 131I-C-NIM2 increases during the following time intervals. The maximum uptake of 131I-C-NIM2 in S. aureus was determined as 5.28 ± 0.54 % at 4 h. In E. coli, 131I-C-NIM2 adhered more than 12.66 ± 1,46 % compared to S. aureus. Similar results were observed to 131I-CNIM1 in 131I-CNIM2, which showed higher uptake in E. coli. Also, similar results were observed with the bacterial uptake results of 131I-NIM3 and 131I-C-NIM3 shown in Fig. 8. 131I-C-NIM3 was shown a higher uptake potential than 131I-NIM3. The maximum uptakes of 131I-NIM3 and 131I-C-NIM3 were determined at 4 h as 2.61 ± 0.27 % and 5.61 ± 0.36 %, respectively in S. aureus. In E. coli, these values increased as the incubation period increased and reached the highest uptake value at the 4th hour in 131I-NIM3 and 131I-C-NIM3, found as 657 ± 1,38 % and 1382 ± 106 respectively. According to these results, as compared to its derivatives, NIM1 showed a higher uptake than the rest by S. aureus bacteria. The nature of the cell wall, ligand, coordination sites, geometry of the compound, positive charge density, hydrophilicity, lipophilicity, presence of co-ligand, pharmacokinetic factors also play important roles in the antimicrobial activity of compounds [54,55].

These properties, as expected, may show antibacterial effects via different mechanisms. For example, when the compounds interact with the phosphate groups of the bacterial cell wall, the cationic charges on the imidazolium ring increase the antimicrobial activity due to the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged ligands and the negatively charged part on the cell wall [54,55]. In other words, the existence of the positive charge on the nitrogen atom in the imidazolium ring may have increased the affinity toward the microbial membrane surface of NIMs [63]. In Gram-negative bacteria, the lipid membrane surrounding the bacterial cell is known to be an important factor to control antimicrobial activity due to lipophilicity [64]. In another study, it was reported that the low antimicrobial activity of some metal complexes is caused by their low lipophilicity, and this could be explained by the decreased penetration of the complex in the lipid membrane. In our study, C-NIMs have positive charges and almost the same lipophilicities. According to the results, NIM1 has the highest uptake out of the three different NIMs, and this result is probably due to the different molecular weights of the molecules. It is known that the affinity of a compound may decrease by increasing molecular weight [54,55].

On the other hand, the uptakes of C-NIMs in S. aureus are doubled the pristine NIMs which shows that CDot presence enhances the membrane penetration of the cargo by as high as 4 fold [65–68]. The antibacterial effect of the carbon-based nanoparticles was proven in several studies in the literature. The antibacterial efficiency of SWCNTs on Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacterial strain was determined the first time by Kang et al. [54]. Arias and Yang demonstrated the antimicrobial activities of SWCNTs and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) bearing different surface groups on Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [69]. It was observed in the present study that imidazolium salt integrated carbon dots (C-NIMs) showed higher uptake efficiency than bare imidazolium salts (NIMs) by S. aureus and E. coli. According to these results, the CDots improved the dispersibility of NIMs by decreasing the lipophilicity. Additionally, CDots were shown to have the potential to increase uptake and thus antibacterial efficiency of NIMs [70].

According to the results presented here, the hybrid structure obtained by integrating NIMs onto CDots highly increased the bacterial uptake of the molecules by S. aureus and E. coli compared to the bare NIMs. The hydrophilicity and radiolabeling efficiency were also altered when NIMs were incorporated onto the CDots surface. Furthermore, the lengths of the alkyl chains of NIMs were demonstrated to have a strong effect on the uptake potential of these nanocarriers where the shortest the chain length the effective the molecule to enter the bacterial cytoplasm. C-NIM1 showed a higher uptake on both bacteria cells as compared to the C-NIM2 and C-NIM3. Although a more detailed study is needed to prove the anti-bacterial potential of C-NIMs in animals, this study is an evident proof of CDots as an effective carrier for NIMs to the Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria that could be used for dual purposes as an antimicrobial and nuclear/fluorescent imaging agent.
